docs.title: Novus, from zero to real projectsStart with a clean hello world. End with a real game and a real app.
docs.text:
Systems language · native binaries · package-managed workflow

This guide is not a vague overview. It walks through installing Novus and Nox, creating a project, using std, writing inline assembly, and understanding how real Novus codebases are structured.

Install Novus Make your first project

docs.title: What this repo covers
docs.text:
First project From nox init to your first compiled binary. Language tour Modules, imports, functions, arrays, strings, overloads, and #if. Inline assembly How Novus uses mov, getreg, syscall, and win_call. Working with std The standard library entrypoint, common helpers, and practical code patterns. Platformer walkthrough Follow the rendering loop, input handling, camera, and window API. Todo app walkthrough See a simple CLI app parse arguments and persist data to disk.

Compatibility snapshot

These docs were written against the currently installed toolchain on this machine:

  • Novus compiler: V0.2.0
  • Novus repo: 753ee3c
  • Nox repo: 632c8be
  • window library: 1.1.1
  • std examples: based on current ecosystem usage in the linked repos

docs.title: Recommended reading order
docs.text:
1. Install Novus and Nox
  2. Your First Project
  3. Language Tour
  4. Working with std
  5. Nox and Libraries
  6. Inline Assembly
  7. Project walkthroughs

docs.title: When to use the AI docs
docs.text:
If you are building tooling, generating Novus code automatically, or teaching an AI agent how to reason about a Novus repo, start with:

  • AI reference
  • root-level AI_GUIDE.md
  • root-level llms.txt

docs.title: Related repositories
docs.text:
• Novus
  • Nox
  • Todo-App
  • platformer-game

docs.title: Compatibility snapshot
docs.text:
This site is meant to be practical, so here is the ecosystem snapshot it was written against.

Component Reference Novus compiler Novus Compiler V0.2.0 Novus repo 753ee3c Nox repo 632c8be window library 1.1.1 Example app: Todo-App current GitHub repo state on 2026-05-25 Example app: platformer-game current GitHub repo state on 2026-05-25

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
Novus and its package ecosystem are still evolving. That means older projects can differ in:

  • import style
  • libraries.conf format
  • package layout
  • whether a library uses flat files or platforms/<os>/<arch>.nov

If something in your local project differs from the examples here, check whether:

  1. your lib/ directory is stale
  2. the project was created before the current Nox layout
  3. you are reading an older package version

The troubleshooting page covers the most common mismatch patterns.

docs.title: AI reference
docs.text:
This page is the HTML counterpart to the root-level AI_GUIDE.md.

Use the root file when you want a single document for agent ingestion. Use this page when you want the same material inside the docs site.

docs.title: Fast rules for AI agents
docs.text:
1. Start from main.nov and libraries.conf.
  2. Check whether imports are aliased or unaliased.
  3. Prefer the package loader (lib/<pkg>/main.nov) unless the project intentionally imports a specific file.
  4. Inspect #if blocks before assuming a symbol exists on every platform.
  5. If compile errors mention missing functions from a library alias, verify the vendored lib/<pkg>/ copy is up to date.

docs.title: Syntax quick sheet
docs.text:
module app;

import lib/std std;

fn main() -> i32 {
    let xs: []i32 = [1, 2, 3];
    std.print(std.to_str(std.len(xs)));
    return 0;
}

docs.title: Builtins worth knowing
docs.text:
• mov
  • getreg
  • syscall
  • ptr
  • array_append
  • len
  • win_call

docs.title: Package quick sheet
docs.text:
Typical packages in this ecosystem:

  • std
  • file_io
  • env
  • process
  • window
  • physics

docs.title: Project references
docs.text:
• Install Novus and Nox
  • Language Tour
  • Inline Assembly
  • Working with std
  • Building the Platformer
  • Building the Todo App

docs.title: Your first project
docs.text: This is the quickest clean path into Novus.

docs.title: Create a project with Nox
docs.text:
nox init my-app
cd my-app

That gives you a starter project with:

my-app/
├── main.nov
├── libraries.conf
└── lib/
    └── std/

nox init already pulls the standard library, so you can start writing code immediately.

docs.title: The starter file
docs.text:
A minimal project usually looks like this:

module my_app;

import lib/std std;

fn main() -> i32 {
    std.print("Hello, world!");
    std.exit(0);
    return 0;
}

docs.title: What each line means
docs.text:
• module my_app; gives the file a module name.
  • import lib/std std; imports the std package and aliases it to std.
  • fn main() -> i32 is the entrypoint.
  • std.exit(0) exits the process cleanly.

docs.title: Compile it
docs.text:
novus main.nov

You should get output in:

build/<target>/

For example on macOS arm64:

build/darwin_arm64/main

Run it:

./build/darwin_arm64/main

docs.title: Add a second function
docs.text:
module my_app;

import lib/std std;

fn greet(name: str) -> void {
    std.print("Hello " + name);
}

fn main() -> i32 {
    greet("Novus");
    std.exit(0);
    return 0;
}

docs.title: What libraries.conf does
docs.text:
Nox keeps your dependencies in libraries.conf.

Modern projects often contain lines like:

installed=std

pkg:std:source=registry
pkg:std:url=https://github.com/MJDaws0n/novus-std
pkg:std:version=1.3.0

This file is important because it tells Nox:

  • which packages are installed
  • where they came from
  • which version to use

docs.title: Pull another package
docs.text:
nox pull window

That places the package into lib/window/ and updates libraries.conf.

At that point your code can import it:

import lib/window/main window;

docs.title: Common beginner mistakes
docs.text:
1. Running novus from the wrong directory.
  2. Forgetting to run nox init first.
  3. Editing code while an old lib/ directory is still checked in from a previous package version.
  4. Mixing old and new import styles without checking the package entrypoint.

docs.title: Inline assembly
docs.text:
One of the distinctive parts of Novus is that low-level work happens inside the language rather than through a separate FFI layer for every platform.

This is mainly useful for:

  • syscalls
  • register manipulation
  • tiny platform backends
  • Windows API calls via win_call

Most application code should still prefer std and existing libraries.

docs.title: The core builtins
docs.text: Builtin What it does mov(reg, value) load a value into a register getreg(reg) read a register value syscall() issue a syscall on Unix-like targets ptr(value) get the address of a value push(value) / pop() direct stack operations win_call("Name", ...) call a Windows API symbol

docs.title: A tiny register example
docs.text:
fn read_back_demo() -> u64 {
    mov(x0, 42);
    return getreg(x0);
}

This does not do any OS work. It just proves that Novus can place and read values directly from registers.

docs.title: A syscall-shaped example
docs.text:
The exact syscall numbers are platform-specific, but the structure is always the same:

fn raw_write(fd: i32, buf: str) -> u64 {
    mov(x0, fd);
    mov(x1, ptr(buf));
    mov(x2, len(buf));
    mov(x16, 0x2000000 + 4);
    syscall();
    return getreg(x0);
}

That is the general pattern used in many Novus low-level libraries:

  1. load arguments into the correct registers
  2. load the syscall number
  3. call syscall()
  4. read the result with getreg(...)

docs.title: Windows API calls
docs.text:
On Windows, Novus libraries often use win_call instead of syscall.

win_call("ExitProcess", 0);

This pattern appears in platform-specific code such as terminal or environment helpers.

docs.title: Conditional compilation matters
docs.text:
Inline assembly is usually wrapped in #if blocks:

#if(os == "darwin") {
    mov(x16, 0x2000000 + 4);
    syscall();
}

#if(os == "windows") {
    win_call("ExitProcess", 0);
}

That is the normal way to keep one public API while swapping implementations per OS or architecture.

docs.title: When to reach for inline assembly
docs.text:
Use it when you are:

  • building a library backend
  • wrapping a platform syscall directly
  • exposing a missing low-level primitive

Do not use it for everyday string handling, arrays, printing, file IO, or process launching if an official library already does that work.

docs.title: Best place to learn from real code
docs.text:
Study these sources:

  • official Novus docs and legacy docs
  • novus-window
  • novus-env
  • novus-process
  • examples/lib/term/main.nov in the Novus repo

docs.title: Install Novus and Nox
docs.text: Novus is the compiler. Nox is the package manager. Install both.

docs.title: Automatic install
docs.text: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/MJDaws0n/novus/main/install.sh | bash

docs.title: Manual install
docs.text:
Download the correct binary from the Novus releases page, then put it on your PATH.

sudo cp novus_<your-platform> /usr/local/bin/novus
sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/novus

# macOS only
sudo xattr -d com.apple.quarantine /usr/local/bin/novus

docs.title: 2. Install Nox
docs.text:
Download a Nox release from the Nox repository, then install it:

sudo cp nox_download /usr/local/bin/nox
sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/nox
sudo xattr -d com.apple.quarantine /usr/local/bin/nox

docs.title: 3. Verify the tools
docs.text:
novus
nox help

If both commands print help or version text, you are ready to create a project.

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
• Install Xcode Command Line Tools.
  • If Gatekeeper blocks a binary you downloaded manually, clear quarantine with xattr -d.

docs.title: Linux
docs.text: Make sure the platform assembler and linker are available for the targets you care about.

docs.title: Cross compilation
docs.text:
If you cross-compile, your host still needs the right backend tools. The Novus README is the best place to check exact requirements for:

  • linux/amd64
  • linux/arm64
  • linux/386
  • windows/amd64
  • darwin/arm64

docs.title: What to do next
docs.text: After installation, go straight to Your First Project. That is the fastest way to confirm the whole toolchain works end to end.

docs.title: Language tour
docs.text: This page is a practical tour of the syntax you actually see in Novus projects.

docs.title: Modules and imports
docs.text:
module example;

import lib/std std;
import lib/window/main window;

docs.title: Variables and basic types
docs.text:
let name: str = "Novus";
let count: i32 = 3;
let big: i64 = 999999;
let ready: bool = true;

The ecosystem strongly prefers explicit integer sizes like i32 and i64.

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
fn greet(name: str) -> void {
    print("Hello " + name);
}

Functions can return values:

fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {
    return a + b;
}

docs.title: Overloading
docs.text:
Novus supports function overloading when parameter types differ.

fn label(value: i32) -> str {
    return "number";
}

fn label(value: str) -> str {
    return "text";
}

docs.title: Conditionals
docs.text:
if (count > 0) {
    print("positive");
}

docs.title: Loops
docs.text:
let i: i32 = 0;
while (i < 3) {
    print("loop");
    i = i + 1;
}

docs.title: Strings
docs.text:
let msg: str = "Hello";
let joined: str = msg + " world";
let size: i32 = len(joined);

docs.title: Arrays
docs.text:
let xs: []i32 = [10, 20, 30];
array_append(xs, 40);
let first: i32 = xs[0];

docs.title: Conditional compilation
docs.text:
Novus uses #if(...) for compile-time platform selection.

#if(os == "darwin") {
    import platforms/darwin/arm64;
}

#if(os == "linux") {
    import platforms/linux/common;
}

You will see this constantly in official libraries.

docs.title: Common conditions
docs.text:
• #if(os == "darwin")
  • #if(os == "linux")
  • #if(os == "windows")
  • #if(arch == "arm64")
  • #if(arch == "amd64")
  • #if(arch == "x86")

docs.title: Builtins versus libraries
docs.text:
Some things are compiler builtins:

  • len
  • array_append
  • mov
  • getreg
  • syscall
  • ptr

Most everyday work should still go through libraries such as std, file_io, window, net, or physics.

docs.title: A complete small example
docs.text:
module example;

import lib/std std;

fn describe(value: i32) -> str {
    return "number " + std.to_str(value);
}

fn describe(value: str) -> str {
    return "text " + value;
}

fn main() -> i32 {
    let xs: []i32 = [1, 2, 3];
    std.print(describe(std.len(xs)));
    std.print(describe("ready"));
    std.exit(0);
    return 0;
}

docs.title: Nox and libraries
docs.text: Nox is what turns Novus from "a compiler you can run once" into a reusable ecosystem.

docs.title: Initialise a project
docs.text:
nox init my-app

That creates the project skeleton and pulls std.

docs.title: Install a registry package
docs.text:
nox pull window
nox pull file_io

Nox downloads packages into lib/<name>/.

docs.title: What libraries.conf is for
docs.text:
This file is the package manifest for the project.

Modern example:

installed=std,file_io

pkg:std:source=registry
pkg:std:url=https://github.com/MJDaws0n/novus-std
pkg:std:version=1.3.0

Older projects may include:

  • branch=...
  • commit=...
  • version ranges

You will still see those in some repositories.

docs.title: Importing installed packages
docs.text:
Once a package is present in lib/, import it from code:

import lib/file_io fio;
import lib/window/main window;

docs.title: Updating projects safely
docs.text:
If a project starts throwing strange errors after a package ecosystem change, check:

  1. the libraries.conf version
  2. the actual code inside lib/<package>/
  3. whether the project has a stale vendored package copy

That specific problem appears often when older projects keep package directories checked into git.

docs.title: What a package should contain
docs.text:
At minimum:

my-lib/
├── main.nov
├── VERSION
├── libraries.conf
└── docs.md

If the library is platform-specific, a structured layout is cleaner:

platforms/
├── darwin/
├── linux/
└── windows/

docs.title: Working with std
docs.text: The standard library is the package almost every Novus project touches first.

docs.title: Import style
docs.text:
The cleanest modern pattern is:

import lib/std std;

In some projects you may also see:

import lib/std/main;

Both styles exist in the current ecosystem. When in doubt, check the package entrypoint in lib/std/main.nov.

docs.title: Printing
docs.text: std.print("hello");

docs.title: Converting values
docs.text:
let count: i32 = 5;
std.print(std.to_str(count));

docs.title: Length helpers
docs.text:
let text: str = "abc";
let xs: []i32 = [1, 2, 3];

std.print(std.to_str(std.len(text)));
std.print(std.to_str(std.len(xs)));

docs.title: Strings and arrays
docs.text:
Novus applications often combine builtins and std-style helpers:

let xs: []str = [];
array_append(xs, "first");
array_append(xs, "second");
std.print(std.to_str(std.len(xs)));

docs.title: Why std matters
docs.text:
std is where application code should start before reaching for inline assembly or platform-specific packages.

It lets you:

  • print and exit cleanly
  • convert numbers to strings
  • work with arrays and strings idiomatically
  • avoid re-implementing basic helpers in every project

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
module hello_std;

import lib/std std;

fn main() -> i32 {
    let title: str = "Novus";
    let nums: []i32 = [4, 8, 15, 16];

    std.print("Title: " + title);
    std.print("Items: " + std.to_str(std.len(nums)));
    std.exit(0);
    return 0;
}

docs.title: Real-world note
docs.text: Older projects may still call helpers like array_len_str(...) or use broad unaliased imports. Modern docs and modern libraries are moving toward a more consistent surface where common operations are unified.

docs.title: Authoring libraries
docs.text: If you want to build a package for Nox, design it so downstream projects can import it cleanly and update it safely.

docs.title: Recommended structure
docs.text:
my-library/
├── main.nov
├── VERSION
├── libraries.conf
├── docs.md
├── tests/
└── platforms/
    ├── darwin/
    ├── linux/
    └── windows/

docs.title: main.nov should be the public loader
docs.text:
That file should expose the library's public surface and select the right backend if needed:

module my_library;

#if(os == "darwin") {
    import platforms/darwin/arm64;
}

#if(os == "linux") {
    import platforms/linux/common;
}

The goal is simple:

  • user code imports one stable entrypoint
  • the library handles platform selection internally

docs.title: libraries.conf
docs.text: Keep the dependency manifest accurate. If your package depends on std, net, env, or anything else, declare it so Nox can resolve dependencies when users install your library.

docs.title: VERSION
docs.text: Include a plain version file so registry updates and release automation can read the current version directly.

docs.title: docs.md
docs.text:
Every package should document:

  • what the library is for
  • how to import it
  • the public functions
  • platform support
  • any required helper binaries or external tools

docs.title: Tests
docs.text: Official low-level libraries should include tests for every supported OS and architecture, especially where inline assembly or platform syscalls are involved.

docs.title: Publishing flow
docs.text:
At a high level:

  1. finish the code and tests
  2. bump VERSION
  3. push the repo
  4. update the Nox registry entry

docs.title: Keep the public API small
docs.text: The best Novus libraries expose a tight public surface and hide platform-specific chaos behind it. window, env, process, and file_io are all better to use when the platform split lives inside the package rather than inside every application.

docs.title: novus or nox command not found
docs.text:
Make sure the binaries are on your PATH, for example:

ls /usr/local/bin/novus
ls /usr/local/bin/nox

docs.title: macOS says the binary cannot be opened
docs.text:
Clear quarantine:

sudo xattr -d com.apple.quarantine /usr/local/bin/novus
sudo xattr -d com.apple.quarantine /usr/local/bin/nox

docs.title: A project compiles against the wrong package API
docs.text:
This usually means the local lib/ folder is stale.

Check:

  1. libraries.conf
  2. the actual code inside lib/<package>/
  3. whether the package version matches the registry

This is especially important for older projects that vendor their library folders in git.

docs.title: A package imports but symbols are missing
docs.text:
Look at the package's main.nov:

  • is it a platform loader?
  • is it importing the right platforms/<os>/<arch>.nov file?
  • is the symbol only defined in a backend file?

If a project imports an old vendored copy of a package, you can get undefined-function errors even when the upstream repo is already fixed.

docs.title: A window-backed app shows black space after resize
docs.text:
That usually means the app is receiving the new content size but the backend framebuffer was not recreated to match it.

The correct behavior is:

  1. the backend tracks the real content size
  2. wm_size(fd) reports it
  3. the app updates its view dimensions
  4. the next frame draws into the larger area

docs.title: A cross-platform build fails
docs.text:
Check two things:

  1. the package's #if loader supports your target
  2. the host has the right assembler/linker toolchain for that target

docs.title: GitHub Pages is not showing the docs
docs.text:
For this repository, Pages should point at:

  • branch: main
  • folder: /docs

If Pages is enabled but you still see an old site, rebuild the docs and push the generated docs/ folder again.

docs.title: Building the platformer
docs.text: The platformer-game is one of the best current examples of a real Novus app that is still small enough to understand in one sitting.

docs.title: What it imports
docs.text:
import lib/window/main window;
import lib/physics/main physics;
import lib/std/main;

That already tells you a lot about the architecture:

  • window handles native windowing and drawing
  • physics handles collision checks
  • std provides general helpers

docs.title: Game state
docs.text:
The project stores almost all state in globals:

  • player position and velocity
  • grounded state
  • camera position
  • life count
  • current mode (playing / win / lose)
  • current view size

That is a very common early-stage Novus pattern: keep the app simple, obvious, and mostly data-driven.

docs.title: Resizing and view size
docs.text:
The platformer asks the window library for the current content size:

fn update_view_size(fd: i32) -> void {
    let size: str = window.wm_size(fd);
    let sep: i32 = str_find(size, "x");
    if (sep < 0) { return; }
    let w: i32 = str_to_i32(substr_len(size, 0, sep));
    let h: i32 = str_to_i32(substr(size, sep + 1));
    if (w >= 320) { view_width = w; }
    if (h >= 240) { view_height = h; }
}

That is what lets the game expand the visible play area instead of just stretching pixels.

docs.title: Update loop
docs.text:
The logic loop is straightforward:

fn update_game(input_mask: i32) -> void {
    frame_index = frame_index + 1;

    if ((input_mask & window.wm_input_quit()) != 0) {
        app_running = 0;
        return;
    }

    if (game_mode != 0) {
        if ((input_mask & window.wm_input_jump()) != 0) {
            restart_game();
        }
        return;
    }

    apply_input(input_mask);
    update_player();
}

Important design point: the game treats input as a bitmask, not as individual callback events.

docs.title: Rendering model
docs.text:
Rendering is explicit rectangle drawing.

fn render_game(fd: i32) -> void {
    window.wm_clear(fd, COLOR_SKY());
    draw_world(fd);
    draw_hud(fd);
    window.wm_present(fd);
}

That is the core frame model:

  1. clear
  2. draw the world
  3. draw the HUD
  4. present

docs.title: Clipping
docs.text:
The project clips every rectangle against the current view size before it reaches the window backend:

fn draw_rect_clipped(fd: i32, x: i32, y: i32, width: i32, height: i32, color: i32) -> void {
    ...
    if (draw_x + draw_w > VIEW_WIDTH()) {
        draw_w = VIEW_WIDTH() - draw_x;
    }
    if (draw_y + draw_h > VIEW_HEIGHT()) {
        draw_h = VIEW_HEIGHT() - draw_y;
    }
    ...
    window.wm_rect(fd, draw_x, draw_y, draw_w, draw_h, color);
}

This is a good example of keeping high-level game logic independent from backend details.

docs.title: Main loop
docs.text:
fn main() -> i32 {
    let fd: i32 = window.wm_open_default("Novus Platformer");
    if (fd < 0) {
        return 1;
    }

    window.wm_resize(fd, VIEW_WIDTH(), VIEW_HEIGHT());
    window.wm_show(fd);
    update_view_size(fd);
    restart_game();

    while (app_running == 1) {
        let input_mask: i32 = window.wm_input(fd);
        update_view_size(fd);
        update_game(input_mask);
        render_game(fd);
        window.wm_sleep_ms(16);
    }

    window.wm_quit(fd);
    return 0;
}

This is a strong example of the normal shape of a Novus application:

  • initialise libraries
  • run a simple main loop
  • keep rendering explicit
  • clean up on exit

docs.title: Why this example matters
docs.text:
The platformer demonstrates:

  • package-based imports
  • a native window API
  • drawing without a heavyweight graphics stack
  • real input handling
  • a simple game loop
  • resize-aware rendering

If you want to learn how Novus applications are really put together, study this repo after you are comfortable with the basics.

docs.title: Building the Todo app
docs.text:
The Todo-App is a good CLI example because it stays simple:

  • parse arguments
  • load a file
  • update data
  • write the file back

docs.title: Imports
docs.text:
import lib/std;
import lib/file_io;

This is an older import style, but it still shows an important point: small Novus applications can be built from only a couple of libraries.

docs.title: Command parsing
docs.text:
The app starts by checking the subcommand:

let supportedOperation: []str =['help', 'list' ,'add', 'check'];
let operation: str = str_lower(argv_get(argv, 1));

if(!array_contains_str(supportedOperation, operation)){
    print('Incorrect Usage');
    printUsage();
    exit(2);
}

That is a classic Novus CLI pattern:

  • read argv
  • normalise input
  • validate against an allowed list

docs.title: Reading tasks
docs.text:
Tasks live in a plain text file and are parsed into an array:

fn getTasks() -> []str {
    let tasksFile: str = getTasksFileLocation();
    if(!file_exists(tasksFile)){
        return [];
    }

    let tasksStr: str = read_file(tasksFile);
    let tasks: []str = [];

    while (tasksStr != '') {
        let task: str = str_split_first(tasksStr, "\n");
        if (task != '') {
            array_append(tasks, task);
        }

        tasksStr = str_split_rest(tasksStr, "\n");
    }

    return tasks;
}

The design is intentionally simple and easy to debug.

docs.title: Writing tasks
docs.text:
Adding a task just appends a new line in the app's custom pipe-delimited format:

fn addTask(newTask: str) -> i32{
    let tasksFile: str = getTasksFileLocation();
    let currentTasks: str = '';

    if(file_exists(tasksFile)){
        currentTasks = read_file(tasksFile) + '\n';
    }

    let taskid: i32 = array_len_str(getTasks()) + 1;
    currentTasks = currentTasks + i32_to_str(taskid) + '|' + newTask + '|0';

    if (write_file(tasksFile, currentTasks) != 0) {
        print('An error occured saving the task');
        exit(1);
    }

    return taskid;
}

docs.title: Toggling completion
docs.text:
The app rewrites the whole file after flipping the task state:

fn checkTask(taskid: i32) -> []str {
    ...
    updatedTasks = updatedTasks + "\n" + taskParts[0] + '|' + taskParts[1] + '|' + taskParts[2];
    ...
    if (write_file(tasksFile, updatedTasks) != 0) {
        print('An error occured saving the task');
        exit(1);
    }
    ...
}

That is not the most sophisticated storage model, but it is a very good learning example because every step is visible.

docs.title: Why this example matters
docs.text:
The Todo app shows how to:

  • build a CLI entrypoint
  • validate arguments
  • read and write plain files
  • use arrays of strings as application state
  • keep an application entirely in Novus

If the platformer shows the graphical side of the ecosystem, the Todo app shows the command-line side.
